title: Coordinating Care for Senior Veterans and Wounded Warriors Act
short_title: Coordinating Care for Senior Veterans and Wounded Warriors Act
plain_summary: The Veterans Affairs department must start a three-year test program to help veterans who get healthcare from both Medicare and the VA. The program will give these veterans case managers who will help them use both healthcare systems more easily. The goal is to make sure veterans get better care, spend less money, and don't miss out on any healthcare they need. This will help senior veterans and wounded warriors get the medical help they deserve from both programs.
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Health.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Coordinating Care for Senior Veterans and Wounded Warriors Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Pilot Program on Coordination of Care Between Department of Veterans Affairs and Medicare Program
sections.text: The Secretary of Veterans Affairs must work with the Secretary of Health and Human Services to create a test program. This test program will help coordinate, guide, and manage care and benefits for veterans who are enrolled in both Medicare and the VA healthcare system. The goals of this test program are to make healthcare better for these veterans in several ways. First, it should make it easier for veterans to get healthcare at VA medical centers, from community care providers that work with the VA, and from doctors who take Medicare. Second, it should improve how well veterans get better from their medical care. Third, it should improve the quality of care veterans receive. Fourth, it should lower the cost of care for veterans. Fifth, it should eliminate gaps where veterans miss needed care and prevent paying twice for the same services. Sixth, it should improve how different doctors work together to care for veterans, including sharing patient information and medical records between providers. The Secretary will run this test program through the Center for Innovation for Care and Payment at the Department of Veterans Affairs. The program will operate in at least three but no more than five Veterans Integrated Service Networks (large regional areas where the VA provides services). These areas must have many veterans who use both Medicare and VA care, and they must include different types of locations. Some locations must be in rural or very rural areas, as determined by the Department of Agriculture's Rural-Urban Commuting Areas system. Some locations must also be in areas that do not have enough medical services available. Each veteran in the test program will be assigned a case manager. This case manager will figure out what each veteran needs for their health and create a personalized care plan with specific treatment goals. The case manager will help veterans get the services they need and help them navigate both the VA system and the Medicare system. When designing this test program, the Secretary should use existing successful models whenever possible. These include value-based care models that commercial health insurance companies use to improve access to care, health outcomes, quality, customer experience, and lower costs per person. The Secretary should try as much as possible to hire private companies that run commercial healthcare programs to help design, implement, and manage care and benefits under this test program. This includes providing care coordination services. If the Secretary decides that hiring private companies is not possible, they must notify the Senate Committee on Veterans Affairs and the House Committee on Veterans Affairs. This notification must explain what determination was made, what steps were taken to try to hire a private company, why hiring a private company is not possible, and how the Secretary plans to run the test program without private companies. This might include using VA employees, other government agency workers, nonprofit organizations, or other types of organizations. The Secretary must track specific measurements during the test program. These include the number of veterans participating broken down by Veterans Integrated Service Network, how much veterans rely on VA healthcare services, how much veterans rely on Medicare services, quality of care including patient outcomes, cost of care, access to care including meeting VA access standards, patient satisfaction, provider satisfaction, and care coordination including timely sharing of information and medical records. When tracking these measurements, the Secretary must also track whether the care a veteran receives is related to a disability caused by military service, which priority group each veteran belongs to in the VA enrollment system, what types of care and services are provided to veterans, and the demographics of veterans in the program including their age. The test program will last for three years starting from when it begins. The Secretary must submit reports to the Senate Committee on Veterans Affairs and the House Committee on Veterans Affairs. During the first two years after this law is passed, the Secretary must submit a report at least every three months about the development, implementation, results, and design of the test program. This includes information about the measurements being tracked. One of these reports must describe the final design of the test program. Starting no later than one year after the final quarterly report, and then at least once every year during the test program, the Secretary must submit a report about the results of the test program. No later than one hundred eighty days before the test program ends, the Secretary must submit a final report about the test program. This final report must include the Secretary's recommendation about whether the test program should be extended or made permanent. In this section, covered veteran means a veteran who is enrolled in both the Medicare program and the VA's annual patient enrollment system. Secretary means the Secretary of Veterans Affairs.
